scenes.text: At the airport and your bag hasn't arrived. You need to get a staff member's attention!
At a Korean restaurant and you're ready to order. Time to call the server over!
Shopping at a market and you want to ask about a product. Catch the staff's eye!

sections.stepTitle: Good news: you already know hundreds of Korean words!
sections.insight: Korean puts the verb at the end of the sentence. It feels different at first, but the pattern never changes — and once you see it, you can build endless sentences from one template.

sections.stepTitle: Swap the word — make a sentence!

character.msg:
Pick a word and
build your sentence!

sections.instruction: "___ juseyo" = "Give me ___." Tap a word to try it! 👇

questions.question: At the airport, how do you get a staff member's attention?
questions.correctMsg: Correct! 저기요 is a universal phrase — use it anywhere you need someone's attention.
questions.wrongMsg: Not quite! The answer is 저기요 (jeo-gi-yo). Think of it as a friendly 'Excuse me~' — works everywhere.

questions.question: At a restaurant, how do you say "Give me this"?
questions.sub: Think back to the pattern practice!
questions.correctMsg: Perfect! 이거 주세요 works at any restaurant, café, or market in Seoul!
questions.wrongMsg: Not quite! The answer is 이거 주세요 (i-geo ju-se-yo). 이거 = this, 주세요 = give me please.

sections.stepTitle: Fun things to know about Korea!

culture.title: Korean restaurants have table call buttons!
culture.body:
You don't need to wave your arm or try to make eye contact — most Korean restaurants have a call bell right on your table!

But when you want to call out, just say:
"저기요～！(jeo-gi-yo!)"
It works exactly like "Excuse me!" in English — friendly and natural.

Also worth knowing: 물 (mul) = water is often self-service at Korean restaurants. Look for the water dispenser near the kitchen or counter.

drama.note: You've definitely heard these in K-dramas! Now you know exactly what they mean ✨
